old_sense: male canine only
new_sense: any canine regardless of sex
explanation: old meaning is a subset of new meaning (scope widened).

old_sense: a young avian (nestling/fledgling)
new_sense: any avian creature
explanation: old meaning is a subset of new meaning (scope widened).

old_sense: a public assembly or court of law
new_sense: any entity, object, or concept
explanation: old meaning is a subset of new meaning (scope widened dramatically).

old_sense: an effigy of Guy Fawkes
new_sense: any man (or person, in plural)
explanation: old meaning is a subset of new meaning (scope widened).

old_sense: a strict 40-day isolation period
new_sense: any period of medical isolation
explanation: old meaning (specifically 40 days) is a subset of the new meaning (any duration).

old_sense: any solid food
new_sense: animal flesh used for food
explanation: new meaning is a subset of old meaning (scope narrowed).

old_sense: to die (from any cause)
new_sense: to die specifically from hunger
explanation: new meaning is a subset of old meaning (scope narrowed).

old_sense: a child or young person of either sex
new_sense: a female child
explanation: new meaning is a subset of old meaning (scope narrowed).

old_sense: any domestic dog
new_sense: a dog bred specifically for hunting
explanation: new meaning is a subset of old meaning (scope narrowed).

old_sense: any wild animal or beast
new_sense: a specific family of hoofed mammals (Cervidae)
explanation: new meaning is a subset of old meaning (scope narrowed).

old_sense: the liquid H₂O
new_sense: the liquid H₂O
explanation: core referential scope unchanged across contexts.

old_sense: hard, solid, non-metallic mineral matter
new_sense: hard, solid, non-metallic mineral matter
explanation: core referential scope unchanged across contexts.

old_sense: a woody perennial plant with a trunk
new_sense: a woody perennial plant with a trunk
explanation: core referential scope unchanged across contexts.

old_sense: a natural, periodic state of rest for the mind and body
new_sense: a natural, periodic state of rest for the mind and body
explanation: core referential scope unchanged across contexts.

old_sense: the natural satellite of the Earth
new_sense: the natural satellite of the Earth
explanation: core referential scope unchanged across contexts.

old_sense: the terminal part of the human arm
new_sense: the terminal part of the human arm
explanation: core referential scope unchanged across contexts.

old_sense: a small rodent
new_sense: a handheld computer pointing device
explanation: shape/tail resembles the cable; different domains (nature vs. tech). Perceptual resemblance motivates the cross-domain leap.

old_sense: a large wading bird with a long neck
new_sense: a machine for lifting heavy loads
explanation: The visual resemblance between the bird's long neck and the machine's boom arm motivated the transfer. Different domains (natural world vs. engineering), linked by perceptual similarity.

old_sense: to scatter seeds widely by hand
new_sense: to transmit radio or television signals
explanation: The image of dispersing outward in all directions (seeds → signals) is a perceptual resemblance across different domains (agriculture vs. media).

old_sense: a visible mass of condensed water vapor in the sky
new_sense: remote servers used for data storage and computing
explanation: The shared quality of being diffuse, overhead, and semi-opaque motivates the cross-domain leap (meteorology → computing).

old_sense: the physical surface of a desk
new_sense: the primary user interface screen of a computer
explanation: The visual layout of a working surface (papers, tools) is mapped onto the digital workspace. Perceptual resemblance across physical and digital domains.

old_sense: a prayer (counting prayers on a rosary)
new_sense: a small sphere object threaded on a string
explanation: beads USED FOR prayer → the physical object itself. Instrument-for-action metonymy within the same devotional event frame.

old_sense: perspiration; the fluid secreted by sweat glands
new_sense: to work hard or labour strenuously
explanation: sweat is the PHYSICAL RESULT of hard work (effect-for-cause metonymy). Both senses inhabit the same bodily/labour event frame.

old_sense: deontic obligation — expressing duty or necessity
new_sense: simple future tense — expressing what will happen
explanation: Modal shift within the same functional domain of futurity (deontic → epistemic). Contiguity-based shift; no domain leap.

old_sense: a hard, brittle, transparent material
new_sense: a drinking vessel or a pair of spectacles
explanation: Material stands for the object made from it (material-for-product metonymy). The referents remain within the same conceptual world.

old_sense: a feudal farm servant or serf
new_sense: a malicious or wicked person
explanation: Class association within a shared social world: the low-status farm worker was stereotyped as untrustworthy, and the social label transferred to the moral quality (contiguity-based shift).

old_sense: the muscular organ in the mouth
new_sense: a language or manner of speaking
explanation: Organ-for-function metonymy: the instrument of speech stands for speech itself. Both senses inhabit the same bodily/communicative event frame.

old_sense: firmly fixed in place, immovable
new_sense: moving or acting quickly
explanation: Both senses share the abstract idea of 'no yielding/resistance' — applied analogically from physical anchoring to speed. No perceptual resemblance; purely structural/functional mapping.

old_sense: boldly, vigorously, with great force
new_sense: scarcely, barely, almost not at all
explanation: Structural shift from degree-of-effort to degree-of-occurrence. The abstract scalar structure is re-mapped across different semantic dimensions.

old_sense: to break down food chemically in the stomach
new_sense: to mentally absorb and process information
explanation: The abstract structure 'input → systematic processing → assimilation' is mapped from the biological domain to the cognitive domain. No perceptual resemblance — purely functional/structural analogy.

old_sense: short for 'motor car' — a self-propelled road vehicle
new_sense: any automobile
explanation: The compound 'motor car' was shortened to 'car', which inherited the full meaning. Diagnostic: the historically attested compound 'motor car' preceded the shortened form.

old_sense: relating to dogs, or specifically a canine tooth
new_sense: a dog (the animal itself)
explanation: 'Canine tooth' or 'canine animal' was shortened; the adjective absorbed the full noun meaning. Diagnostic: the compound phrase preceded the standalone use.

old_sense: short for 'omnibus' — a vehicle serving all (Latin: for all)
new_sense: any large road vehicle for carrying passengers
explanation: The compound 'omnibus' was shortened to 'bus', which then inherited the full meaning of the longer phrase. Diagnostic: a historically attested longer form ('omnibus') preceded the shortened word.

old_sense: a woman of noble birth and refined manners
new_sense: a prostitute (via ironic euphemism)
explanation: The phrase was used sarcastically/ironically to refer to its opposite. The shift is socially driven — ironic inversion in context, not a genuine reversal.

old_sense: morally evil or sinful
new_sense: excellent, impressive (informal)
explanation: The positive sense arose through ironic inversion in informal/slang contexts: speakers used the negative word to express strong approval. The shift is socially/ironically driven, not a literal polarity reversal.

old_sense: evil, wicked, of poor quality
new_sense: excellent, impressive (slang)
explanation: The word shifted to mean its opposite through slang/register shift. No ironic framing required — speakers genuinely use it positively.

old_sense: to split or sever apart
new_sense: to cling or adhere firmly to
explanation: The word developed two directly opposite meanings (split apart vs. stick together) through internal linguistic drift, with no ironic framing required. Speakers genuinely use both senses.

old_sense: a piece of canvas used to catch wind and propel a boat
new_sense: a sailing vessel as a whole
explanation: Part-for-whole transfer: the most visually prominent part (the sail) comes to stand for the entire ship. Both senses remain within the same domain (maritime).
